themes.name: Rabbit Holes Worth Falling Down
themes.tagline: From ancient battlefields to forgotten shoes — history's weird corners are calling.

items.title: Eric Cline on The REAL Armageddon
items.description:
Armageddon is more than just a biblical prophecy hailing the end of days. It is a real place: Megiddo, an ancient city that for thousands of years stood at the crossroads of empires, trade routes and wars in the ancient Near East.

In this episode of The Ancients, Tristan Hughes is joined by friend of the show Eric Cline to uncover the true story of the real Armageddon. Why did Megiddo become associated with the world’s final battle? What made this city so strategically important for millennia?

items.title: What We Discovered about these 18th Century Shoes
items.description:
In the 18th Century, highly decorative silk-covered heels were fashionable footwear for the wealthy, both men and women. This pair of pink shoes were recently acquired by Historic Royal Palaces for conservation.

In this video, Textile Conservator Katica Laza discusses the features of these beautiful shoes, and demonstrates how pieces like this are restored in our Conservation Studio. As 18th Century damage is repaired, more information about the shoes is discovered. To properly conserve the sho

items.title: How Astronaut Food Has Changed Over Time
items.description:
Everyone knows that traveling into space raises a lot of difficult issues, for example: How do you feed a human in zero gravity? Crumbs can damage equipment, liquids float away, and every meal has to survive launch while providing enough nutrition to keep astronauts alive millions of miles from Earth. 

In this episode of Weird History Food, we explore the strange, surprising history of space food, from freeze-dried meals to the science behind eating in orbit. Discover how NASA solved one of the

items.title: The Stokes Twins Answer The Web's Most Searched Questions
items.description:
The Stokes Twins visit WIRED to answer their most searched questions on Google. Can the Stokes Twins speak Chinese? Do the Stokes Twins have any world records? Are the Stokes Twins on Netflix? Answers to these questions and many more await on the Stokes Twins’ WIRED Autocomplete Interview.

Fans can now stream The Stokes Twins on Netflix with an archive of their most-watched content to date. Next up, the twins are expanding their storytelling into long-form content for audiences worldwide with a

morning_soundtrack.title: The War and Treaty: Tiny Desk Concert
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The husband-wife duo, and their tight band, deliver fresh, soul-inflected songs of uncommon power.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Church-raised and road-hardened, this is music that grabs you by the collar and insists you feel something.

morning_soundtrack.title: Nicole Atkins – “Singing In The Mirror”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: The Nashville-based singer-songwriter Nicole Atkins has been making really good music for a long time. It's the kind of sharp, classicist pop music that's easy to take for granted, but you shouldn't do that. Every time I hear a new Atkins song, I'm surprised at how good it is. At this point, I shoul
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The kind of song you rediscover on a playlist and pause whatever you're doing to actually listen.

morning_soundtrack.title: Eels – “All Forgotten” & “I’m On Standby”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Los Angeles fixture Mark Oliver Everett has been making permanently bummed-out orchestral alt-pop for decades. Right now, Everett has a few projects in various stages of completion, including a Judd Apatow-produced documentary and an upcoming album called Cookie Happened. We've already heard lead si
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Sitting alone in a dimly lit kitchen at 2am, not sad exactly, just quietly acquainted with sadness.

morning_soundtrack.title: Melaina Kol – “Idola”
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Nashville-based artist Logan Hornyak, who makes tangled glitch-folk music as Melaina Kol, is releasing his new album Okay that’s a great idea because if I do that then, his first for Philly label Julia's War, in a few weeks. Yesterday, he released the new single "Idola," which is another captivating
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like walking through a familiar forest and realizing the trees have quietly rearranged themselves.

morning_soundtrack.title: QUEER COUNTRY: All Aboard the Pride Party Boat: OUTlaw Pride Announces New Record Label
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Austin's Julie Nolen is used to building things herself. Hailing from West Texas, Nolen has cultivated a rough-and-tumble country sound, showcased in her 2016 album Songs of Dignity and Grit. Both of these ingredients are needed to keep OUTlaw Pride going — what started as a
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Boot-stomping defiance with a rainbow flag flying out the truck window on a long Texas highway.

morning_soundtrack.title: Listen to an Unvaulted John Prine Song From Archival Album Found Dogs
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Five unearthed songs and several alternate versions comprise the LP, celebrating the 30th anniversary of Lost Dogs & Mixed Blessings
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like finding an old handwritten letter tucked inside a book you forgot you loved.

morning_soundtrack.title: Stef Chura Recruits Peter Buck, Mike Mills for New Album
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Dancing Alone on the Concrete is out October 2 on Saddle Creek
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Late-night indie rock with the restless ache of someone dancing alone in an empty apartment.

morning_soundtrack.title: Unreleased John Prine Recordings to Be Compiled In New Collection Found Dogs
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
The collection features outtakes and more from 1995's Lost Dogs & Mixed Blessings.
Unreleased John Prine Recordings to Be Compiled In New Collection Found Dogs
Chris Coplan
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: A dusty treasure chest cracked open, letting the warm, bittersweet light of a lost voice spill back into the room.

morning_soundtrack.title: Lizzy McAlpine Is Lovestruck on Majestic New Song “The light in the painting”: Stream
morning_soundtrack.snippet:
Angel, McAlpine's fourth album, arrives on September 18th.
Lizzy McAlpine Is Lovestruck on Majestic New Song “The light in the painting”: Stream
Paolo Ragusa
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The breathless, overexposed feeling of being newly in love when everything looks slightly too beautiful to be real.

morning_soundtrack.title: “Eve of Destruction”: History Never Repeats Itself in These 12 Versions
morning_soundtrack.snippet: From the Vietnam War and mid-1960s sociopolitical turmoil to its 21st-century repurposing, P. F. Sloan's folk-rock classic "Eve of Destruction" retains its eerie power.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The unsettling comfort of an old protest song that sounds like it was written this morning.

morning_soundtrack.title: Guitarist Miles Okazaki Expands His Sound with ‘Boomtown’
morning_soundtrack.snippet: Jazz artist Miles Okazaki has had no trouble defining his own space and personality on the big American landscape. He sounds, finally, like no other guitarist.
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: The sonic equivalent of a city skyline at dusk — densely layered, quietly ambitious, and entirely its own.

global_silver_linings.title: Maiden Rocket Launch ‘a Grand Success’ for India’s First Private Spaceflight Company
global_silver_linings.snippet: In 2022, India beat long-time spacefarers Russia to become the 3rd nation to see a privately designed and built rocket successfully reach space. Now just 4 years later, and the Hyderabad-based Skyroot Aerospace has outdone itself. The company’s new 4-stage Vikram-1 rocket capable of carrying over 770 lbs.
global_silver_linings.reason: India's first private spaceflight company reaching orbit is a landmark moment that opens the door to broader, more diverse participation in space exploration beyond a handful of powerful nations. When more of humanity has a stake in reaching the stars, the ambition and ingenuity driving that effort grows with it.

global_silver_linings.title: The upside of the global energy crisis
global_silver_linings.snippet: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has brought renewed urgency to the climate solutions campaigners have long championed The post The upside of the global energy crisis appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: Crisis has a way of forcing the change that comfort never would, and the global energy crunch is accelerating the shift toward renewables faster than any policy alone could have managed. The painful disruption is quietly laying the groundwork for a more resilient, independent energy future.

global_silver_linings.title: What It Means to Shape Your Own Space After Prison
global_silver_linings.snippet: Two cubes of pale wood sit on either side of a small, accessible lounge. Inside each, a bed folds down from the wall, a desk swings out and shelves hold books, plants and family photos. But nobody lives here — at least, not yet.
global_silver_linings.reason: People rebuilding their lives after incarceration deserve spaces that feel like their own, and programs helping them do exactly that restore dignity in one of the most concrete ways possible. It's a reminder that genuine second chances are built not just with words, but with walls, rooms, and a place to call home.

discovery_articles.title: The Mary Pit Head in Fife, Scotland
discovery_articles.snippet: The Kingdom of Fife has a rich mining history, but nowadays, most of it is gone. The concrete frame of the No 2 Pit at Mary Colliery, informally known as "Big Mary" (to differentiate from "Wee Mary", a nearby competitor), remains one of only two reminders of the coal mines in this area, standing 80 foot high. Adjacent to the structure is a small outdoor exhibit which showcases equipment from the time, such as coal-cutters, hydraulic roof supports, and carts (both for coal and for miners) that were used in the pit.

discovery_articles.title: The Payphone at the Edge of the Forest in Altadena, California
discovery_articles.snippet: At the edge of the forest in Altadena, a payphone. Not just any payphone, a working payphone. A relic like this would be easy to miss anywhere in Los Angeles.

discovery_articles.title: La Casa del Serpentello in Vignale Monferrato, Italy
discovery_articles.snippet: What makes the Serpentello House truly special is its unique artistic character. The façade and many of its decorations are entirely covered with handcrafted ceramic tiles, each one unique and personally designed and created by the owner, Luciano Serpentello, in 1925. This remarkable attention to detail transforms the house into a true work of art, giving it a distinctive and unforgettable atmosphere.

discovery_articles.title: Their Names Were Lost to History. But Now, You Can See What Their Faces May Have Looked Like When They Lived in a Roman-Era City Nearly 2,000 Years Ago
discovery_articles.snippet: A new exhibition in Budapest features 16 facial reconstructions based on skulls excavated from the ancient city of Aquincum. Curators don't know the details of their lives, but they've invented backstories to fill in the gaps

discovery_articles.title: Hunter-Gatherers Brought Trout to a Norwegian Mountain Lake 7,000 Years Ago, the Earliest Known Example of Humans Translocating Fish
discovery_articles.snippet: Researchers have long known that the animals were introduced into the region's high-altitude bodies of water, but they weren't sure when it happened or whether humans had a hand. Analyses of recently discovered ancient fish traps provide new clues

discovery_articles.title: Would You Chat With an A.I. 'Ghost' of a Deceased Loved One? New Research Suggests That the Tech Can Provide Comfort and Closure
discovery_articles.snippet: Commercial "griefbots" are becoming more popular, and many experts have questions about their psychological and ethical impacts. A new study aims to ensure that the bots are designed responsibly

discovery_articles.title: Before Cultural and Natural Wonders Earn UNESCO World Heritage Status, They Are Put on a 'Tentative' List. Here Are Five Nominees Worth Visiting Now
discovery_articles.snippet: Featuring more than 1,800 sites from 181 countries and territories, UNESCO’s tentative list is a treasure trove of lesser-known marvels

discovery_articles.title: All the Queens houses
discovery_articles.snippet: To the trained eye, the personalised homes of Queens, NYC, evoke everything from a Venetian streetscape to a Chinese temple - by Aeon Video Watch on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: The savage bites back
discovery_articles.snippet: Rooted in Brazilian modernism, anthropophagy devours and transforms culture, subverting colonial fears of cannibalism - by Sofia Cándano Read on Aeon

discovery_articles.title: The Secret Lives of Insects
discovery_articles.snippet: Can fruit flies have dialects? Can dung beetles navigate by the stars? Find surprising answers in this collection of stories about bugs and people.

discovery_articles.title: How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding?
discovery_articles.snippet: One of the most contested issues in astrophysics is our understanding of the Universe’s expansion. Adam Riess discusses why there is disagreement and what might resolve it. The post How Fast Is the Universe Really Expanding?

discovery_articles.title: Fields and Abacus Medals 2026
discovery_articles.snippet: Every four years, the math world’s preeminent awards are given to the finest minds of a generation. The young winners — all under 40 on the first day of the year — have reshaped mathematics and theoretical computer science. Here are their stories.

discovery_articles.title: A Master of the Traveling Salesperson Problem Finds His Own Path
discovery_articles.snippet: Shayan Oveis Gharan has won the Abacus Medal for using tools from across mathematics to boost the power of algorithms. The post A Master of the Traveling Salesperson Problem Finds His Own Path first appeared on Quanta Magazine

discovery_articles.title: Living Fully in the Math World Means Threading the Needle
discovery_articles.snippet: Focus, commitment, and insight resulted in a “once in a century” proof. Hong Wang is now just the third woman to win a Fields Medal. The post Living Fully in the Math World Means Threading the Needle first appeared on Quanta Magazine

discovery_articles.title: Patrick Dougherty’s ‘Stickwork’ Installations Conjure Connections with Nature
discovery_articles.snippet: For more than four decades, the artist has reimagined the technical and sculptural capabilities of one simple material: twigs. Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.

featured_read.title: Carl Jung on Creativity
featured_read.snippet: The question of what it takes to create — to make something of beauty and substance that touches other lives across space and time — is one of the deepest, oldest questions, perhaps because the answer to it is so unbearably simple: everything. We bring everything we are and everything we have lived to every smallest creative act — every experience, every dream, every memory, every unremembered impression, every unconsciously absorbed influence. The great bewilderment is that we can only access a fraction of our own everythingness — most of it dwells in the recesses of the mind and the… read article
featured_read.blurb: Jung's uncomfortable answer to what creativity actually costs us is that it demands the whole self — including the parts we've forgotten we carry. This piece sits quietly with the paradox that our deepest creative material lives precisely where we can't deliberately reach it.

garden_note.note: The long Zürich evening stretches out luxuriously, the garden still warm from a July day that feels almost too generous — this is the moment to deadhead the dahlias and pull a few courgettes before they become marrows overnight. Linger a little; dusk won't settle until nearly ten.
in_season: Deadheading lavender to encourage a second flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: The first quarter moon hangs bright in the south after sunset — look just below it for the faint teapot shape of Sagittarius low on the horizon.
garden_note.sun_times: The sun slips away at 21:13 tonight, with dusk lingering on until 21:48.

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
previous_puzzle.prompt: I always follow you around, everywhere you go at night. I look very bright to people, but I can make the sun dark. I can be in many different forms and shapes. What am I?

fern_data.greeting: Tonight the air has that particular high-summer thickness — still warm, faintly electric — that makes an unvaulted John Prine song feel like finding a letter you didn't know someone wrote you, and makes a video of forbidden colors feel, somehow, completely plausible.
fern_data.top_pick_title: Warm Air, Strange Light, Found Things
